title: Raised Intracranial Pressure
oneLiner: A life-threatening state of increased pressure within the cranial vault, most commonly from trauma, mass, or impaired CSF dynamics, requiring urgent recognition and treatment to prevent herniation and death.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic Vignette:**
A 45-year-old man with a history of hypertension and a recent fall from height presents to the Emergency Department. He was initially alert but over the past hour has become progressively drowsy and now responds only to painful stimuli. His pulse is 50 bpm, blood pressure is 175/95 mmHg, and his left pupil is dilated and non-reactive to light. He has a GCS of 9 (E2 V2 M5). His right arm is held in flexion and both legs extend stiffly to pain.

**Dissection of pattern clues:**
- *Progressive drowsiness after trauma* → rising ICP compressing brainstem arousal centres
- *Cushing’s reflex (bradycardia, hypertension)* → brainstem ischaemia triggers sympathetic surge and baroreceptor response – a late, ominous sign
- *Unilateral fixed, dilated pupil* → transtentorial herniation compressing CN III (parasympathetic fibres) against the petroclinoid ligament
- *Decorticate (flexor) posturing on one side, decerebrate on the other* → compression of the corticospinal tract at or below the level of the red nucleus; suggests brainstem involvement
- *GCS < 8* → inability to protect airway, high risk of aspiration and secondary brain injury

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- *Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH) in obese young women*: No focal signs, only headache, papilloedema, and sometimes visual obscurations – no mass, no trauma, but pressure is elevated.
- *Subtle cognitive decline in elderly with normal-pressure hydrocephalus*: Classic triad (gait apraxia, urinary incontinence, dementia) but ICP may be normal on LP – yet treatable with shunt.
- *Burst suppression or false normalisation after sedation*: In the ICU, a sedated patient may not show Cushing's reflex until hermiation is imminent; look for loss of pupillary reactivity or asymmetric motor response to stimulation.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
Raised intracranial pressure (ICP) is any sustained elevation above 20 mmHg in the pressure within the skull (normal ~5–15 mmHg in adults supine). Because the cranial vault is a fixed volume (the Monro-Kellie doctrine), an increase in one component (brain, blood, CSF) must be compensated by a decrease in others – once compensation fails, pressure rises exponentially.

Why it matters: ICP elevation is the final common pathway for many neurological and neurosurgical emergencies – traumatic brain injury, intracranial haemorrhage, meningitis, hydrocephalus, and mass lesions. Untreated, it leads to brain herniation and death. It is common: ~50% of severe TBI patients have intracranial hypertension. Missing it is catastrophic – the window for intervention can be minutes. Pattern recognition of early signs (headache, vomiting, papillodema) is as important as managing the acute crisis.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story:** The skull is a rigid box containing three essentially incompressible components – brain (80%), blood (12%), and CSF (8%). When a new volume is added (e.g., haematoma, tumour, oedema), compensation occurs first by displacing CSF into the spinal canal and by compressing venous sinuses. Once these buffers are exhausted (pressure-volume curve shifts to a steep exponential), even a small additional volume (like a 5mL haematoma) causes a large rise in ICP.

**Why each key sign happens:**

- **Headache** – activation of pain-sensitive structures: dura, blood vessels, cranial nerves by stretching or distortion. Worse in morning (recumbency increases ICP) and with coughing/straining (increases venous pressure).
- **Papilloedema** – elevated CSF pressure in the optic nerve sheath impairs axoplasmic transport in the retinal ganglion cells; the optic disc swells, edges blur, and venous pulsations disappear. It takes hours to days to develop – *absent papilloedema does not rule out acute raised ICP*.
- **Vomiting** – direct stimulation of the area postrema (chemoreceptor trigger zone) at the floor of the fourth ventricle, often projectile and without prior nausea.
- **Cushing’s reflex** – brainstem ischaemia from decreased cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP = MAP – ICP) stimulates sympathetic outflow (hypertension) and then a baroreceptor-mediated bradycardia (due to increased vagal tone). This is a **late, pre-hermiation** sign – by the time you see it, herniation is imminent.
- **Altered consciousness** – compression of the reticular activating system in the upper brainstem. Level of consciousness is the most sensitive clinical monitor of ICP.
- **Pupillary asymmetry** – the third nerve runs along the edge of the tentorium cerebelli; when the uncus of the temporal lobe herniates, it compresses CN III, first affecting parasympathetic fibres (dilated, fixed pupil) then motor fibres (ptosis, “down and out” position).
- **Posturing** – damage to descending pathways: decorticate (flexion of upper limbs, extension of lower) suggests lesion above the red nucleus (cortex/internal capsule); decrebrate (extension of all four) indicates damage at or below the red nucleus (midbrain/pons) – more ominous.

**Connecting to pattern clues:** In the classic vignette, the fall caused a subdural haematoma (volume added), which exhausted compensation → progressive drowsiness → Cushing’s reflex → unilateral dilated pupil from uncal hermiation → posturing from brainstem compression. The GCS drop tracks the rise in ICP.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Traumatic** | Epidural haematoma (arterial bleed – middle meningeal artery) | Lucid interval after head injury, then rapid decline |
| | Subdural haematoma (venous bleeding from bridging veins) | Elderly, alcoholics, anticoagulant use – slow decline, often with headache |
| | Cerebral contusion / oedema | Diffuse axonal injury with delayed swelling |
| **Spontaneous haemorrhage** | Subarachnoid haemorrhage (aneurysm) | “Thunderclap” headache, neck stiffness, photophobia |
| | Intraparenchymal haemorrhage (hypertensive) | Acute focal deficit, vomiting, hypertension |
| **Mass lesion** | Tumour (primary or metastatic) | Gradual headache, seizure, focal signs, morning worsening |
| | Abscess (bacterial, fungal) | Fever, signs of infection, immunocompromised, sinusitis |
| **Infectious** | Meningitis (bacterial, TB, cryptococcal) | Fever, neck stiffness, photophobia, altered mental state |
| | Encephalitis (e.g., HSV) | Fever, seizure, behavioural change, temporal lobe signs |
| **Cerebrospinal fluid disturbance** | Hydrocephalus (obstructive vs communicating) | Gait disturbance, incontinence, dementia (normal pressure hydrocephalus); vomiting, sunsetting eyes (infants) |
| **Idiopathic** | Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH) | Obese young woman, headache, pulsatile tinnitus, visual blurring; no mass on imaging |
| **Metabolic / toxic** | Hepatic encephalopathy, hyponatraemia | Asterixis, jaundice, hyponatremia – cause cerebral oedema |
| **Venous outflow obstruction** | Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis | Headache, seizure, focal signs in young women (OCP, pregnancy), often with signs of raised ICP |

**Most common cause overall:** Traumatic brain injury (especially in younger patients), followed by spontaneous intracerebral haemorrhage (in older hypertensive patients).
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:** No single threshold is absolute. **Sustained ICP > 20 mmHg** (normal 5–15) is the accepted treatment threshold for intervention. In the absence of an ICP monitor, clinical signs and imaging guide management.

**Initial imaging:**
- **CT head (non-contrast)** – first-line, <30 min. Look for:
  - Mass effect (midline shift >5mm is serious, >10mm is critical)
  – Effacement of sulci, basal cisterns, ventricles (loss of compensatory space)
  – Hyperdense lesions (acute blood), hypodense (oedema, infarct)
  – “Swinging” or “pineal gland shift” – sign of significant pressure
- **CT venogram** if suspected venous sinus thrombosis
- **MRI brain** – if CT negative but high suspicion (e.g., early ischaemia, posterior fossa lesions, venous thrombosis, hypoxic injury)

**Invasive monitoring:**
- **External ventricular drain (EVD)** – gold standard for ICP measurement + therapeutic CSF drainage
- **Intraparenchymal or subdural bolt** – used when EVD not needed

**What single finding confirms?** A sustained ICP >20 mmHg via an invasive monitor is the gold standard. On imaging: **loss of basal cisterns** (especially perimesencephalic) is a strong predictor of high ICP and imminent herniation.

**What rules out?** A CT head that shows normal ventricles, no mass effect, and open basal cisterns makes severe raised ICP unlikely in the acute setting. But remember: early (especially hyperacute) CT may appear normal; repeat scanning or clinical progression dictates management.

**Other tests:**
- **Fundoscopy** – Look for papilloedema, absent venous pulsation (high specificity but low sensitivity for acute rise)
- **Lumbar puncture** – **Contraindicated with known or suspected mass lesion or obstructive hydrocephalus** (risk of tonsillar herniation). Only safe in IIH and suspected chronic meningitis after imaging shows no mass.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) – the “ABCDE” of brain resuscitation:**

- **Airway & Breathing:** GCS <8 → intubate and ventilate. **Target PaCO₂ 4.5–5.0 kPa (normocapnia)** – profound hyperventilation (PaCO₂ <4 kPa) should be avoided except as a temporary bridging measure (minutes) because it causes cerebral vasoconstriction and can worsen ischaemia. **Why:** prevents hypoxia (secondary injury) and controls PaCO₂-induced vasodilation.
- **Circulation:** Maintain **MAP at 80-90 mmHg** to ensure cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP = MAP – ICP) > 60 mmHg. **Avoid hypotension** (SBP <90) – worse outcomes. **If ICP known, target CPP 60–70 mmHg.**
- **Elevate head of bed** to 30° – facilitates cerebral venous drainage.
- **Osmotherapy:** **Mannitol 0.25–1 g/kg IV over 20 min** (20% solution) or **Hypertonic saline 3% 150-250 mL IV** (3% NaCl). **Why:** creates osmotic gradient to draw water out of brain parenchyma. **Pitfall:** Mannitol can cause hypovolaemia and acute kidney injury; monitor serum osmolality (keep <320 mOsm/L). Hypertonic saline is preferred in hypotensive patients (expands plasma volume).
- **Sedation & Analgesia:** Propofol or midazolam + fentanyl to reduce metabolic demand and prevent coughing/straining which spikes ICP.
- **Seizure prophylaxis:** Levetiracetam 500-1000 mg IV (now preferred over phenytoin due to fewer side effects) – seizures increase ICP dramatically.

**What NOT to do:** Do NOT give large volume hypotonic fluids (e.g., 0.45% NaCl) – worsens cerebral oedema. Do NOT perform lumbar puncture if there is suspicion of mass (risk of herniation). Do NOT hyperventilate to PaCO₂ <4 kPa prophylactically – it reduces ICP but also reduces cerebral blood flow causing ischaemia.

**Ward (24–72 hours) – definitive management & monitoring:**

- **Continuation of head elevation, normocapnia, sedation as needed.**
- **ICP monitor insertion** if GCS <8 and CT shows mass effect – the Brain Trauma Foundation guidelines recommend ICP monitoring in severe TBI.
- **CSF drainage** via EVD if hydrocephalus present – drain 5-10 mL/hour to keep ICP below 20 mmHg.
- **Treat the cause:**
  - Haematoma >30 mL in supratentorial region or >10 mL in posterior fossa → surgical evacuation.
  - Brain abscess → antibiotics + aspiration.
  - Hydrocephalus → EVD or shunt.
  - IIH → acetazolamide (500 mg PO BID) + diet/exercise; repeated lumbar punctures; optic nerve sheath fenestration if vision threatened.
- **Seizure monitoring** – continuous EEG if unexplained fluctuations in ICP or consciousness.
- **Corticosteroids:** **Only in vasogenic oedema from tumour or abscess** (dexamethasone 8-16 mg/day in divided doses). **Contraindicated in TBI** (increases mortality – CRASH trial) and in stroke.
- **Nutrition** – enteral feeding via nasogastric tube within 24-48h; avoid overfeeding (increases CO₂ production → cerebral vasodilation).
- **DVT prophylaxis** – low molecular weight heparin once haemostasis confirmed (48-72h after trauma/surgery), with sequential compression devices.

**Long-term:**

- **Sequelae management** – rehabilitation, neuropsychological support, treatment of post-traumatic epilepsy (if seizures occur).
- **Shunt dependency** – if hydrocephalus was treated with shunting, long-term follow-up for malfunction or infection.
- **IIH** – weight loss (target >10% body weight), acetazolamide for 6-12 months, monitor visual fields.
- **Tumour** – follow-up imaging, consideration of ongoing radiotherapy/chemotherapy.

**Drug doses summary (inline):**
- Mannitol 20%: 0.25–1 g/kg IV over 20 min, repeat q4-6h PRN; max 2g/kg/day
- Hypertonic saline 3%: 150-250 mL IV over 20 min; titrate to serum Na+ 150-155
- Levetiracetam: 500-1000 mg IV loading, then 500-1000 mg IV/PO BID
- Dexamethasone: 8-16 mg/day PO/IV in divided doses (0.5-0.75 mg/kg/day)
- Acetazolamide: 500 mg PO BID (IIH)
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Raised ICP | Migraine | Meningitis | Subarachnoid Haemorrhage | Benign Intracranial Hypertension (IIH) |
|---------|------------|----------|------------|--------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| Pain | Dull, worse supine, morning, with straining | Pulsatile, unilateral, with aura | Severe, global, with photophobia, neck stiffness | Thunderclap, worst ever | Diffuse, moderate, pulsatile tinnitus |
| Exam | Papilloedema, CN III palsy, Cushing's, posturing | No focal signs, possible visual aura | Meningism, fever, may have rash | Neck stiffness, photophobia, CN III / VI palsy | Papilloedema, normal neuro except possible visual field loss |
| CT head | Midline shift, effaced cisterns, mass / bleed | Normal | Normal (early) or hydrocephalus | Hyperdense blood in cisterns/sulci | Normal (empty sella sign subtle) |
| LP | Contraindicated unless ruled out mass – high ICP | Normal | CSF: ↑ WBC, ↓ glucose, ↑ protein | CSF: xanthochromia, ↑ RBCs, ↑ bilirubin | High opening pressure (>25 cmH₂O), normal CSF |
| Key differentiator | Cushing’s reflex, CN III palsy, >20 mmHg ICP | No fever, no neck stiffness, normal LP | Fever + CSF culture positive | Thunderclap onset + CT hyperdense | Obese young female, normal imaging, high LP opening pressure |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
- **Transtentorial (uncal) herniation:** a medial temporal lobe (uncus) is pushed into the tentorial notch, compressing CN III (dilated fixed pupil), then the ipsilateral posterior cerebral artery (occipital lobe infarction), and finally the brainstem (bilateral decerebrate posturing, apnoea, death). **What to do:** immediate mannitol/hypertonic saline, secure airway, neurosurgical decompression.
- **Central herniation:** symmetrical downward displacement of the diencephalon through the tentorial notch. Progresses from drowsiness (early), to small reactive pupils and Cushing's (mid), to fixed midposition pupils and flaccid coma (late). **Same immediate actions.**
- **Tonsillar (foramen magnum) herniation:** cerebellar tonsils herniate through the foramen magnum, compressing the medulla → cardiorespiratory arrest. **Emergency: intubate, hyperventilate (briefly), emergency suboccipital decompression.**
- **Brainstem ischaemia and death** – if CPP drops below 30 mmHg for >15 minutes, irreversible injury.
- **Secondary brain injury** – from hypoxia, hypotension, seizures, fever – each increases ICP, causing a vicious cycle.
- **Long-term:**
  - Visual loss from papilloedema (IIH)
  - Cognitive impairment, epilepsy

**Late-presentation pattern:** A patient found unconscious with bilateral fixed and dilated pupils, absent oculocephalic reflex (no Doll’s eye), and no motor response to pain – this is brainstem failure consistent with herniation; proceed with aggressive management if pupillary response was present within 5 minutes of arrest, but prognosis is extremely poor.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic – “CUSHING” for signs of rising ICP:**
- C – Consciousness reduced
- U – Unilateral pupillary dilation (CN III)
- S – Systolic hypertension (with bradycardia)
- H – Headache (early)
- I – Irregular breathing (late – brainstem compression)
- N – Nausea/vomiting (projectile, no prior nausea)
- G – GCS drop (1-2 points is a red flag)

**Mnemonic – “MONRO-KELLIE” for compensatory mechanisms:**
- M – Minimising CSF volume (displacement to spinal canal)
- O – Outflow of venous blood (compression of sinuses)
- N – No more space (once exhausted, rapid pressure rise)
- R – Reduces with hyperventilation (brief)
- O – Osmotherapy (mannitol/hypertonic saline) works by dehydrating brain

**Clinical pearls:**
1. *The best monitor of ICP is the pupil and the GCS, not the ICP monitor.* In a patient with an ICP monitor reading of 20, but with a non-reactive dilated pupil, they are herniating.
2. *“If the cisterns are gone, the pressure is high.”* Loss of the basal cisterns on CT (especially peripontine and crural) is the most reliable imaging sign of critical ICP.
3. *The Cushing triad (bradycardia, hypertension, irregular breathing) is a LATE sign.* About 30% of patients with severe ICP elevation never develop it. Do not wait for it to act.
4. *Hyperventilation to PaCO₂ <4.5 kPa can be used for minutes as a bridge to surgery, but prolonged use causes ischaemia.*
5. *In a patient with GCS <8, intubate first.* A compromised airway worsens ICP through hypoxia and hypercapnia.
6. *Seizures can mimic elevated ICP (posturing, autonomic instability) and also cause it.* Treat seizures aggressively.
7. *Never do an LP if there is focal neurological deficit or papilloedema suggestive of mass lesion – you can cause herniation.* Always image first.

**Common mistakes:**
- Assuming a normal CT rules out raised ICP (low-grade oedema, early ischaemia, IIH).
- Giving mannitol without ensuring euvolemia (causes AKI and hypotension).
- Over-sedating and losing the ability to clinically monitor progress.
- Failing to escalate early when GCS drops by 2 points.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
| Trigger | Action |
|---------|--------|
| GCS drop by ≥2 points (any drop) | Immediate senior review (Registrar/Consultant) |
| New pupillary asymmetry (any difference >1mm) | Urgent CT head + neurosurgery consult |
| Bradycardia + hypertension (Cushing’s) | Crash call – imminent herniation |
| ICP > 25 mmHg despite initial therapy | Escalate to ICU/neurosurgery for decompression |
| PaCO₂ > 6.0 kPa or PaO₂ < 8.0 kPa (on ABG) | Optimize ventilation; consider prone position if ARDS |
| Serum Na+ > 155 (from hypertonic saline) | Stop hypertonic saline, recheck osmolality |
| Lactate > 4 mmol/L (not explained by seizures/PTA) | Consider hypotension / sepsis – worsens secondary injury |
| NEWS2 ≥ 7 or any single red parameter (resp rate ≤8, O₂ sat ≤91%, systolic BP ≤90) | CALL 2222 – likely deterioration requiring critical care |

**When to call ICU:** For invasive monitoring (EVD/bolt), ongoing sedation/ventilation, or if patient requires >0.5 mg/kg/h propofol to control ICP.

**When to call neurosurgery:** For evacuation of haemorrhage >30 mL (supratentorial) or >10 mL (posterior fossa), or for EVD insertion, or for any sign of herniation.

**Crash team (2222) criteria:** GCS <8 with airway compromise, or unresponsive with extensor posturing / fixed dilated pupils, or cardiorespiratory arrest.

rawMarkdown:
## Raised Intracranial Pressure

**One-liner:** A life-threatening state of increased pressure within the cranial vault, most commonly from trauma, mass, or impaired CSF dynamics, requiring urgent recognition and treatment to prevent herniation and death.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic Vignette:**
A 45-year-old man with a history of hypertension and a recent fall from height presents to the Emergency Department. He was initially alert but over the past hour has become progressively drowsy and now responds only to painful stimuli. His pulse is 50 bpm, blood pressure is 175/95 mmHg, and his left pupil is dilated and non-reactive to light. He has a GCS of 9 (E2 V2 M5). His right arm is held in flexion and both legs extend stiffly to pain.

**Dissection of pattern clues:**
- *Progressive drowsiness after trauma* → rising ICP compressing brainstem arousal centres
- *Cushing’s reflex (bradycardia, hypertension)* → brainstem ischaemia triggers sympathetic surge and baroreceptor response – a late, ominous sign
- *Unilateral fixed, dilated pupil* → transtentorial herniation compressing CN III (parasympathetic fibres) against the petroclinoid ligament
- *Decorticate (flexor) posturing on one side, decerebrate on the other* → compression of the corticospinal tract at or below the level of the red nucleus; suggests brainstem involvement
- *GCS < 8* → inability to protect airway, high risk of aspiration and secondary brain injury

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- *Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH) in obese young women*: No focal signs, only headache, papilloedema, and sometimes visual obscurations – no mass, no trauma, but pressure is elevated.
- *Subtle cognitive decline in elderly with normal-pressure hydrocephalus*: Classic triad (gait apraxia, urinary incontinence, dementia) but ICP may be normal on LP – yet treatable with shunt.
- *Burst suppression or false normalisation after sedation*: In the ICU, a sedated patient may not show Cushing's reflex until hermiation is imminent; look for loss of pupillary reactivity or asymmetric motor response to stimulation.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

Raised intracranial pressure (ICP) is any sustained elevation above 20 mmHg in the pressure within the skull (normal ~5–15 mmHg in adults supine). Because the cranial vault is a fixed volume (the Monro-Kellie doctrine), an increase in one component (brain, blood, CSF) must be compensated by a decrease in others – once compensation fails, pressure rises exponentially.

Why it matters: ICP elevation is the final common pathway for many neurological and neurosurgical emergencies – traumatic brain injury, intracranial haemorrhage, meningitis, hydrocephalus, and mass lesions. Untreated, it leads to brain herniation and death. It is common: ~50% of severe TBI patients have intracranial hypertension. Missing it is catastrophic – the window for intervention can be minutes. Pattern recognition of early signs (headache, vomiting, papillodema) is as important as managing the acute crisis.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story:** The skull is a rigid box containing three essentially incompressible components – brain (80%), blood (12%), and CSF (8%). When a new volume is added (e.g., haematoma, tumour, oedema), compensation occurs first by displacing CSF into the spinal canal and by compressing venous sinuses. Once these buffers are exhausted (pressure-volume curve shifts to a steep exponential), even a small additional volume (like a 5mL haematoma) causes a large rise in ICP.

**Why each key sign happens:**

- **Headache** – activation of pain-sensitive structures: dura, blood vessels, cranial nerves by stretching or distortion. Worse in morning (recumbency increases ICP) and with coughing/straining (increases venous pressure).
- **Papilloedema** – elevated CSF pressure in the optic nerve sheath impairs axoplasmic transport in the retinal ganglion cells; the optic disc swells, edges blur, and venous pulsations disappear. It takes hours to days to develop – *absent papilloedema does not rule out acute raised ICP*.
- **Vomiting** – direct stimulation of the area postrema (chemoreceptor trigger zone) at the floor of the fourth ventricle, often projectile and without prior nausea.
- **Cushing’s reflex** – brainstem ischaemia from decreased cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP = MAP – ICP) stimulates sympathetic outflow (hypertension) and then a baroreceptor-mediated bradycardia (due to increased vagal tone). This is a **late, pre-hermiation** sign – by the time you see it, herniation is imminent.
- **Altered consciousness** – compression of the reticular activating system in the upper brainstem. Level of consciousness is the most sensitive clinical monitor of ICP.
- **Pupillary asymmetry** – the third nerve runs along the edge of the tentorium cerebelli; when the uncus of the temporal lobe herniates, it compresses CN III, first affecting parasympathetic fibres (dilated, fixed pupil) then motor fibres (ptosis, “down and out” position).
- **Posturing** – damage to descending pathways: decorticate (flexion of upper limbs, extension of lower) suggests lesion above the red nucleus (cortex/internal capsule); decrebrate (extension of all four) indicates damage at or below the red nucleus (midbrain/pons) – more ominous.

**Connecting to pattern clues:** In the classic vignette, the fall caused a subdural haematoma (volume added), which exhausted compensation → progressive drowsiness → Cushing’s reflex → unilateral dilated pupil from uncal hermiation → posturing from brainstem compression. The GCS drop tracks the rise in ICP.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Traumatic** | Epidural haematoma (arterial bleed – middle meningeal artery) | Lucid interval after head injury, then rapid decline |
| | Subdural haematoma (venous bleeding from bridging veins) | Elderly, alcoholics, anticoagulant use – slow decline, often with headache |
| | Cerebral contusion / oedema | Diffuse axonal injury with delayed swelling |
| **Spontaneous haemorrhage** | Subarachnoid haemorrhage (aneurysm) | “Thunderclap” headache, neck stiffness, photophobia |
| | Intraparenchymal haemorrhage (hypertensive) | Acute focal deficit, vomiting, hypertension |
| **Mass lesion** | Tumour (primary or metastatic) | Gradual headache, seizure, focal signs, morning worsening |
| | Abscess (bacterial, fungal) | Fever, signs of infection, immunocompromised, sinusitis |
| **Infectious** | Meningitis (bacterial, TB, cryptococcal) | Fever, neck stiffness, photophobia, altered mental state |
| | Encephalitis (e.g., HSV) | Fever, seizure, behavioural change, temporal lobe signs |
| **Cerebrospinal fluid disturbance** | Hydrocephalus (obstructive vs communicating) | Gait disturbance, incontinence, dementia (normal pressure hydrocephalus); vomiting, sunsetting eyes (infants) |
| **Idiopathic** | Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH) | Obese young woman, headache, pulsatile tinnitus, visual blurring; no mass on imaging |
| **Metabolic / toxic** | Hepatic encephalopathy, hyponatraemia | Asterixis, jaundice, hyponatremia – cause cerebral oedema |
| **Venous outflow obstruction** | Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis | Headache, seizure, focal signs in young women (OCP, pregnancy), often with signs of raised ICP |

**Most common cause overall:** Traumatic brain injury (especially in younger patients), followed by spontaneous intracerebral haemorrhage (in older hypertensive patients).

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:** No single threshold is absolute. **Sustained ICP > 20 mmHg** (normal 5–15) is the accepted treatment threshold for intervention. In the absence of an ICP monitor, clinical signs and imaging guide management.

**Initial imaging:**
- **CT head (non-contrast)** – first-line, <30 min. Look for:
  - Mass effect (midline shift >5mm is serious, >10mm is critical)
  – Effacement of sulci, basal cisterns, ventricles (loss of compensatory space)
  – Hyperdense lesions (acute blood), hypodense (oedema, infarct)
  – “Swinging” or “pineal gland shift” – sign of significant pressure
- **CT venogram** if suspected venous sinus thrombosis
- **MRI brain** – if CT negative but high suspicion (e.g., early ischaemia, posterior fossa lesions, venous thrombosis, hypoxic injury)

**Invasive monitoring:**
- **External ventricular drain (EVD)** – gold standard for ICP measurement + therapeutic CSF drainage
- **Intraparenchymal or subdural bolt** – used when EVD not needed

**What single finding confirms?** A sustained ICP >20 mmHg via an invasive monitor is the gold standard. On imaging: **loss of basal cisterns** (especially perimesencephalic) is a strong predictor of high ICP and imminent herniation.

**What rules out?** A CT head that shows normal ventricles, no mass effect, and open basal cisterns makes severe raised ICP unlikely in the acute setting. But remember: early (especially hyperacute) CT may appear normal; repeat scanning or clinical progression dictates management.

**Other tests:**
- **Fundoscopy** – Look for papilloedema, absent venous pulsation (high specificity but low sensitivity for acute rise)
- **Lumbar puncture** – **Contraindicated with known or suspected mass lesion or obstructive hydrocephalus** (risk of tonsillar herniation). Only safe in IIH and suspected chronic meningitis after imaging shows no mass.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour) – the “ABCDE” of brain resuscitation:**

- **Airway & Breathing:** GCS <8 → intubate and ventilate. **Target PaCO₂ 4.5–5.0 kPa (normocapnia)** – profound hyperventilation (PaCO₂ <4 kPa) should be avoided except as a temporary bridging measure (minutes) because it causes cerebral vasoconstriction and can worsen ischaemia. **Why:** prevents hypoxia (secondary injury) and controls PaCO₂-induced vasodilation.
- **Circulation:** Maintain **MAP at 80-90 mmHg** to ensure cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP = MAP – ICP) > 60 mmHg. **Avoid hypotension** (SBP <90) – worse outcomes. **If ICP known, target CPP 60–70 mmHg.**
- **Elevate head of bed** to 30° – facilitates cerebral venous drainage.
- **Osmotherapy:** **Mannitol 0.25–1 g/kg IV over 20 min** (20% solution) or **Hypertonic saline 3% 150-250 mL IV** (3% NaCl). **Why:** creates osmotic gradient to draw water out of brain parenchyma. **Pitfall:** Mannitol can cause hypovolaemia and acute kidney injury; monitor serum osmolality (keep <320 mOsm/L). Hypertonic saline is preferred in hypotensive patients (expands plasma volume).
- **Sedation & Analgesia:** Propofol or midazolam + fentanyl to reduce metabolic demand and prevent coughing/straining which spikes ICP.
- **Seizure prophylaxis:** Levetiracetam 500-1000 mg IV (now preferred over phenytoin due to fewer side effects) – seizures increase ICP dramatically.

**What NOT to do:** Do NOT give large volume hypotonic fluids (e.g., 0.45% NaCl) – worsens cerebral oedema. Do NOT perform lumbar puncture if there is suspicion of mass (risk of herniation). Do NOT hyperventilate to PaCO₂ <4 kPa prophylactically – it reduces ICP but also reduces cerebral blood flow causing ischaemia.

**Ward (24–72 hours) – definitive management & monitoring:**

- **Continuation of head elevation, normocapnia, sedation as needed.**
- **ICP monitor insertion** if GCS <8 and CT shows mass effect – the Brain Trauma Foundation guidelines recommend ICP monitoring in severe TBI.
- **CSF drainage** via EVD if hydrocephalus present – drain 5-10 mL/hour to keep ICP below 20 mmHg.
- **Treat the cause:**
  - Haematoma >30 mL in supratentorial region or >10 mL in posterior fossa → surgical evacuation.
  - Brain abscess → antibiotics + aspiration.
  - Hydrocephalus → EVD or shunt.
  - IIH → acetazolamide (500 mg PO BID) + diet/exercise; repeated lumbar punctures; optic nerve sheath fenestration if vision threatened.
- **Seizure monitoring** – continuous EEG if unexplained fluctuations in ICP or consciousness.
- **Corticosteroids:** **Only in vasogenic oedema from tumour or abscess** (dexamethasone 8-16 mg/day in divided doses). **Contraindicated in TBI** (increases mortality – CRASH trial) and in stroke.
- **Nutrition** – enteral feeding via nasogastric tube within 24-48h; avoid overfeeding (increases CO₂ production → cerebral vasodilation).
- **DVT prophylaxis** – low molecular weight heparin once haemostasis confirmed (48-72h after trauma/surgery), with sequential compression devices.

**Long-term:**

- **Sequelae management** – rehabilitation, neuropsychological support, treatment of post-traumatic epilepsy (if seizures occur).
- **Shunt dependency** – if hydrocephalus was treated with shunting, long-term follow-up for malfunction or infection.
- **IIH** – weight loss (target >10% body weight), acetazolamide for 6-12 months, monitor visual fields.
- **Tumour** – follow-up imaging, consideration of ongoing radiotherapy/chemotherapy.

**Drug doses summary (inline):**
- Mannitol 20%: 0.25–1 g/kg IV over 20 min, repeat q4-6h PRN; max 2g/kg/day
- Hypertonic saline 3%: 150-250 mL IV over 20 min; titrate to serum Na+ 150-155
- Levetiracetam: 500-1000 mg IV loading, then 500-1000 mg IV/PO BID
- Dexamethasone: 8-16 mg/day PO/IV in divided doses (0.5-0.75 mg/kg/day)
- Acetazolamide: 500 mg PO BID (IIH)

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Raised ICP | Migraine | Meningitis | Subarachnoid Haemorrhage | Benign Intracranial Hypertension (IIH) |
|---------|------------|----------|------------|--------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| Pain | Dull, worse supine, morning, with straining | Pulsatile, unilateral, with aura | Severe, global, with photophobia, neck stiffness | Thunderclap, worst ever | Diffuse, moderate, pulsatile tinnitus |
| Exam | Papilloedema, CN III palsy, Cushing's, posturing | No focal signs, possible visual aura | Meningism, fever, may have rash | Neck stiffness, photophobia, CN III / VI palsy | Papilloedema, normal neuro except possible visual field loss |
| CT head | Midline shift, effaced cisterns, mass / bleed | Normal | Normal (early) or hydrocephalus | Hyperdense blood in cisterns/sulci | Normal (empty sella sign subtle) |
| LP | Contraindicated unless ruled out mass – high ICP | Normal | CSF: ↑ WBC, ↓ glucose, ↑ protein | CSF: xanthochromia, ↑ RBCs, ↑ bilirubin | High opening pressure (>25 cmH₂O), normal CSF |
| Key differentiator | Cushing’s reflex, CN III palsy, >20 mmHg ICP | No fever, no neck stiffness, normal LP | Fever + CSF culture positive | Thunderclap onset + CT hyperdense | Obese young female, normal imaging, high LP opening pressure |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

- **Transtentorial (uncal) herniation:** a medial temporal lobe (uncus) is pushed into the tentorial notch, compressing CN III (dilated fixed pupil), then the ipsilateral posterior cerebral artery (occipital lobe infarction), and finally the brainstem (bilateral decerebrate posturing, apnoea, death). **What to do:** immediate mannitol/hypertonic saline, secure airway, neurosurgical decompression.
- **Central herniation:** symmetrical downward displacement of the diencephalon through the tentorial notch. Progresses from drowsiness (early), to small reactive pupils and Cushing's (mid), to fixed midposition pupils and flaccid coma (late). **Same immediate actions.**
- **Tonsillar (foramen magnum) herniation:** cerebellar tonsils herniate through the foramen magnum, compressing the medulla → cardiorespiratory arrest. **Emergency: intubate, hyperventilate (briefly), emergency suboccipital decompression.**
- **Brainstem ischaemia and death** – if CPP drops below 30 mmHg for >15 minutes, irreversible injury.
- **Secondary brain injury** – from hypoxia, hypotension, seizures, fever – each increases ICP, causing a vicious cycle.
- **Long-term:**
  - Visual loss from papilloedema (IIH)
  - Cognitive impairment, epilepsy

**Late-presentation pattern:** A patient found unconscious with bilateral fixed and dilated pupils, absent oculocephalic reflex (no Doll’s eye), and no motor response to pain – this is brainstem failure consistent with herniation; proceed with aggressive management if pupillary response was present within 5 minutes of arrest, but prognosis is extremely poor.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonic – “CUSHING” for signs of rising ICP:**
- C – Consciousness reduced
- U – Unilateral pupillary dilation (CN III)
- S – Systolic hypertension (with bradycardia)
- H – Headache (early)
- I – Irregular breathing (late – brainstem compression)
- N – Nausea/vomiting (projectile, no prior nausea)
- G – GCS drop (1-2 points is a red flag)

**Mnemonic – “MONRO-KELLIE” for compensatory mechanisms:**
- M – Minimising CSF volume (displacement to spinal canal)
- O – Outflow of venous blood (compression of sinuses)
- N – No more space (once exhausted, rapid pressure rise)
- R – Reduces with hyperventilation (brief)
- O – Osmotherapy (mannitol/hypertonic saline) works by dehydrating brain

**Clinical pearls:**
1. *The best monitor of ICP is the pupil and the GCS, not the ICP monitor.* In a patient with an ICP monitor reading of 20, but with a non-reactive dilated pupil, they are herniating.
2. *“If the cisterns are gone, the pressure is high.”* Loss of the basal cisterns on CT (especially peripontine and crural) is the most reliable imaging sign of critical ICP.
3. *The Cushing triad (bradycardia, hypertension, irregular breathing) is a LATE sign.* About 30% of patients with severe ICP elevation never develop it. Do not wait for it to act.
4. *Hyperventilation to PaCO₂ <4.5 kPa can be used for minutes as a bridge to surgery, but prolonged use causes ischaemia.*
5. *In a patient with GCS <8, intubate first.* A compromised airway worsens ICP through hypoxia and hypercapnia.
6. *Seizures can mimic elevated ICP (posturing, autonomic instability) and also cause it.* Treat seizures aggressively.
7. *Never do an LP if there is focal neurological deficit or papilloedema suggestive of mass lesion – you can cause herniation.* Always image first.

**Common mistakes:**
- Assuming a normal CT rules out raised ICP (low-grade oedema, early ischaemia, IIH).
- Giving mannitol without ensuring euvolemia (causes AKI and hypotension).
- Over-sedating and losing the ability to clinically monitor progress.
- Failing to escalate early when GCS drops by 2 points.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

| Trigger | Action |
|---------|--------|
| GCS drop by ≥2 points (any drop) | Immediate senior review (Registrar/Consultant) |
| New pupillary asymmetry (any difference >1mm) | Urgent CT head + neurosurgery consult |
| Bradycardia + hypertension (Cushing’s) | Crash call – imminent herniation |
| ICP > 25 mmHg despite initial therapy | Escalate to ICU/neurosurgery for decompression |
| PaCO₂ > 6.0 kPa or PaO₂ < 8.0 kPa (on ABG) | Optimize ventilation; consider prone position if ARDS |
| Serum Na+ > 155 (from hypertonic saline) | Stop hypertonic saline, recheck osmolality |
| Lactate > 4 mmol/L (not explained by seizures/PTA) | Consider hypotension / sepsis – worsens secondary injury |
| NEWS2 ≥ 7 or any single red parameter (resp rate ≤8, O₂ sat ≤91%, systolic BP ≤90) | CALL 2222 – likely deterioration requiring critical care |

**When to call ICU:** For invasive monitoring (EVD/bolt), ongoing sedation/ventilation, or if patient requires >0.5 mg/kg/h propofol to control ICP.

**When to call neurosurgery:** For evacuation of haemorrhage >30 mL (supratentorial) or >10 mL (posterior fossa), or for EVD insertion, or for any sign of herniation.

**Crash team (2222) criteria:** GCS <8 with airway compromise, or unresponsive with extensor posturing / fixed dilated pupils, or cardiorespiratory arrest.
